coredata.dc:title: Growth-promoting mechanism and biological activity of weed-derived carbon dots on Chinese cabbage and peas
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ni
Tang, Jiaxin
Lai, Jie
Chen, Lihan
Xu, Yanqiu
Bian, Qiang
Zhang, Zan
Zhang, Huanhuan
Qian, Kun
coredata.dc:description: In agriculture, Carbon Dots (CDs) have been shown to enhance plant photosynthesis, boost antioxidant capacity and increase stress resistance. Additionally, CDs can serve as pesticide carrier, to improve the stability, solubility and targeted delivery of pesticide molecules. In this study, novel weed-based CDs derived from Commelina communis were developed. Using peas, Chinese cabbage, and aphids as the test objects, the CDs can promote the growth of seedling, within a certain concentration range, where they can significantly increase the fresh weight of plants. This growth-promoting effect is mainly due to the facilitation of iron ion absorption and the synthesis of chlorophyll by the CDs, thereby enhancing plant photosynthesis. In addition, CDs-flonicamid nanocomposite can improve the adhesion of pesticides on the leaf surfaces, and enhance the control effect against aphids. This work lays a foundation for the development of innovative pesticide formulation based on the CDs and their broader application in agriculture.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Weed-based carbon dots
Plants
Flonicamid
Nano-formulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of CDs on plant growth
Determination of acute toxicity of CDs to earthworms
Analysis of inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer
Toxicity determination of different formulations of flonicamid solution on pea aphids
Measurement of contact angles and adhesion work values of different formulations of flonicamid solution
Physiological and environmental impact of CDs
Effect of CDs on plant growth
Absorption and transport of CDs in plants
Determination results of acute toxicity of CDs to earthworms
Analysis of Fe element content and chlorophyll content in plants
Determination results of protective enzyme activity
Toxicity assessment of different formulations of flonicamid solution on pea aphid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of CDs. (A) Carbon dot synthesis and bioassay schematic diagram. (B) The morphology of CDs under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp and the AFM image of CDs. (C) The zeta potential diagram of CDs. (D) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (E) Emission and excitation spectra of CDs. (F) FL spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (G) FL lifetime spectrum of CDs. (H) XPS spectrum of CDs. (I)-(K) C1S, N1S and O1S spectra of CDs. (L) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (M) XRD spectrum of CDs.

Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on the growth of peas. (Ⅰ-A) Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on pea root length. (Ⅰ-B) Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on pea leaves. (Ⅱ-A) Effect of CDs on root length of peas. (Ⅱ-B) Effect of CDs on plant height of peas. (Ⅱ-C) Effect of CDs on fresh weight of peas. (Different letters indicate significant differences, P < 0.05, Using LSD testing in One way ANOVA, the same below.).

Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on the growth of Chinese cabbage. (Ⅰ-A) Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on root length of Chinese cabbage. (Ⅰ-B) Effects of different concentrations of CDs solution on the leaves of Chinese cabbage. (Ⅱ-A) Effect of CDs on root length of Chinese cabbage. (Ⅱ-B) Effect of CDs on plant height of Chinese cabbage. (Ⅱ-C) Effect of CDs on fresh weight of Chinese cabbage.

Confocal micrograph of root, stem and leaf cross section of pea and Chinese cabbage exposed to CDs solution. (Ⅰ) Confocal micrograph of root, stem and leaf cross section of pea exposed to CDs solution. (Ⅱ) Confocal micrograph of root, stem and leaf cross section of Chinese cabbage exposed to CDs solution.

Survival rate of earthworms treated with CDs solutions, the content of Fe and chlorophyll and the effects of carbon dots treatment on the activities of SOD, POD and CAT in Chinese cabbage. (A) Survival rate of earthworms treated with CDs solutions of different concentrations. (B) Fe content in Chinese cabbage after treatment with 500 mg/L CDs solution. (Different letters indicate significant differences, P < 0.05, Using LSD testing in One way ANOVA) (C) Chlorophyll content in leaves of Chinese cabbage treated with CDs solution. (D) Effect of CDs treatment on superoxide dismutase activity of Chinese cabbage. (E) Effect of CDs treatment on peroxidase activity of Chinese cabbage. (F) Effect of CDs treatment on catalase activity of Chinese cabbage.

Contact angle and adhesion work of different formulations of flonicamid solution. (A) Contact angle diagram of CDs-Flo. (B) Contact angle diagram of flonicamid water dispersible granules. (C) Contact angle diagram of flonicamid technical. (D) Variation of adhesion work with time of flonicamid solution of different dosage forms.
Toxicity determination of flonicamid solution of different dosage forms to pea aphids.
The contact angle of flonicamid solution of different formulations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different letters indicate significant differences, P < 0.05, Using LSD testing in One way ANOVA.

head.ce:title: Growth-promoting mechanism and biological activity of weed-derived carbon dots on Chinese cabbage and peas

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In agriculture, Carbon Dots (CDs) have been shown to enhance plant photosynthesis, boost antioxidant capacity and increase stress resistance. Additionally, CDs can serve as pesticide carrier, to improve the stability, solubility and targeted delivery of pesticide molecules. In this study, novel weed-based CDs derived from Commelina communis were developed. Using peas, Chinese cabbage, and aphids as the test objects, the CDs can promote the growth of seedling, within a certain concentration range, where they can significantly increase the fresh weight of plants. This growth-promoting effect is mainly due to the facilitation of iron ion absorption and the synthesis of chlorophyll by the CDs, thereby enhancing plant photosynthesis. In addition, CDs-flonicamid nanocomposite can improve the adhesion of pesticides on the leaf surfaces, and enhance the control effect against aphids. This work lays a foundation for the development of innovative pesticide formulation based on the CDs and their broader application in agriculture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive and prolonged utilization of pesticides has led to increasingly prominent significant negative impacts on both the environment and human health. Therefore, the agricultural sector urgently requires the advancement of more effective pest management strategies. Within this context, novel green nanomaterials, particularly carbon-based nanomaterials, are emerging as a focal point of research due to their potential positive effects on agricultural production. To safeguard ecological integrity and ensure sustainable agricultural development while addressing pest management challenges, it is imperative to align innovative strategies with green environmental principles and actively advance ecofriendly agricultural technologies (Li et al., 2023).

Carbon nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, and CDs, are composed entirely of carbon (Huang et al., 2023). In recent years, an increasing body of research has demonstrated that these materials can significantly enhance agricultural productivity (Chen et al., 2022; Chandel et al., 2022). Studies indicate that carbon nanomaterials can be absorbed by plants and transported to various tissues and organs, resulting in improved seed germination rates and enhanced plant growth (Cañas et al., 2008; Yan et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2022; Khodakovskaya et al., 2009). Furthermore, these materials function as highly efficient carriers for the delivery of plant growth hormones, nutrients, and pesticides. The unique fluorescent properties of carbon nanomaterials also enable them to detect pesticide residues and other toxic substances (Su et al., 2021). As research on carbon nanomaterials progresses, their role in agriculture continues to expand.

Currently, there has been growing interest in utilizing biomass-derived materials, including carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins, as sources for the synthesis of CDs. For example, Nugroho et al. (2024) successfully produced CDs from Cassia fistula flowers through a hydrothermal method, which were then combined with zinc oxide to form an effective photocatalyst. Rajapandi et al. (2023) synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using Ziziphus mauritiana, demonstrating significant efficacy in both dye degradation and antibacterial activities. Wang et al. (2016) synthesized high quantum yield CDs from wool via a one-step microwave approach. They demonstrated that silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) can quench the fluorescence of these CDs due to an internal filtering effect, enabling the construction of a CDs/AgNP fluorescence system for detecting glyphosate levels. Research indicates that CDs synthesized from biomass exhibit outstanding physical and chemical properties (Mankoti et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024), are environmentally benign, and offer significant sustainability advantages. Commelina communis, a weed known for its robust adaptability and invasive nature, is commonly found in environments such as roadsides, field edges, and grasslands (Dagne et al., 2024). This plant not only contains a high carbon content but is also abundant in bioactive compounds like flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids (Orni et al., 2019). These characteristics make Commelina communis an ideal candidate for CD production. Leveraging this weed for CD preparation could lead to economic benefits while simultaneously aiding in its ecological management and control.

CDs have demonstrated beneficial effects on plant growth, even at low concentrations (Li et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). These nanomaterials also shield chlorella from ultraviolet radiation and oxidative damage by boosting the plants' stress resistance (Su et al., 2018). According to the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), over 96 % of earth’s land surface has been affected by various abiotic stresses such as low cold, drought, soil salinity, UV exposure, pollution, and improper use of pesticides. These factors lead to an estimated loss of nearly half of the world’s annual agricultural production (Cramer et al., 2011). Reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are harmful derivatives of stress metabolism, significantly contribute to abiotic stress in plants. The buildup of ROS within cells often causes oxidative harm to proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, and DNA (Choudhury et al., 2017; Gill and Tuteja, 2010). Plants naturally contain protective enzyme systems that neutralize ROS and alleviate abiotic stress. Studies indicate that CDs can increase the activity of essential enzymes like superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and catalase (CAT), thereby enhancing plants’ ability to eliminate ROS, reducing abiotic stress, and improving their antioxidant capabilities and overall stress tolerance (Li et al., 2017, 2019).

In this research, a unique type of CD was synthesized from dayflower (Commelinae Herba) biomass through a straightforward hydrothermal technique. Following thorough analysis, the biological activities and mechanisms of these CDs were investigated. Results indicated that they are exceptionally eco-friendly, capable of enhancing Fe element absorption in plants and boosting chlorophyll synthesis in leaves, as well as increasing the activity of protective enzymes within plants. Consequently, these benefits promote plant growth and enhance their antioxidant capabilities. Utilizing these CDs as carriers for the insecticide flonicamid, a carbon dot-flonicamid nanoformulation (CDs-Flo) was constructed. When comparing the insecticidal effectiveness of three flonicamid formulations, CDs-Flo demonstrated the highest mortality rate against pea aphids, highlighting the capability of CDs to significantly amplify flonicamid’s pesticidal efficiency. This study reveals that the synthesized CDs not only foster plant development but also markedly improve pesticide performance. These findings unveil new possibilities for the application of CDs in agricultural production and pesticide delivery systems.

Chinese cabbage seeds used in this experiment were purchased from Qingdao Jiao Yan seed and seedling Co., Ltd., pea seeds were purchased from Hebei Dayu Seed Industry Co., Ltd., and broad beans were purchased from Taigu Pangda Seed Co., Ltd., which all were sealed and dried at room temperature. Earthworms used in this experiment were purchased from Xinyida Earthworm Farm and pre-reared for 7 days. Adult earthworms over 2 months old, weighing about 300–600 mg, and with obvious clitella were selected for the experiment. The aphid used in this experiment was pea aphid, which was reared in the preparation processing room of the College of Plant Protection, Southwest University. Potted pesticide-free insect-free broad bean plants were used for feeding. When most of the nymphs produced by them developed into adult aphids, they were collected for testing.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Accurately weighed 0.1–3.5 g of Commelina communis stirred into homogenate, added 10 mL of deionized water, and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor at 160°C-280°C for 6–14 h. The sample was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min, and the supernatant was taken and filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain a clear yellow CD solution. The prepared CDs solution was poured into a three-necked flask, and 0.1 mL of 20 % PEI solution was added drop by drop. The CDs solution modified by PEI was obtained by stirring in an oil bath at 80°C for 4 h. After freeze-drying, the CDs solid was obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C.

Fluorescence spectrophotometer is capable of detecting the fluorescence spectrum emitted by liquid-phase fluorescent markers. In this experiment, 3 mL CD solution was placed in a cuvette, and the sensitivity of the fluorescence spectrophotometer was set to level three. By scanning the solution with various excitation wavelengths, the optimal excitation wavelength for the CDs was identified. Subsequently, by comparing the fluorescence intensities of different CD solutions at this optimal excitation wavelength, the most effective formula for the CDs was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: CDs solutions were prepared at concentrations of 10, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000 and 2000 mg/L for subsequent experiments. A total of 500 pea seeds and 500 Chinese cabbage seeds, selected for uniform size, were surface-sterilized using a 10 % sodium hypochlorite solution. Following a 10-minutes treatment, the seeds were thoroughly rinsed 3–5 times with deionized water. After being soaked in water for 12 hours, the seeds were transferred into petri dishes lined with cotton and gauze. An adequate amount of deionized water was added, and the seeds were covered with black gauze to promote germination under controlled conditions.

Upon germination, when the seed tip s had turned white, 15 pea and Chinese cabbage seeds of uniform size were selected and placed in a 12 cm × 12 cm petri dish with two layers of filter paper. CD solutions, prepared at various concentrations, were added to each dish at a volume of 15 mL per dish, while deionized water without CDs served as the blank control. Each concentration was subjected to three replicates. The dishes were subsequently incubated in a constant-temperature culture chamber set at 25°C. For the first three days, the seeds were kept in the dark, followed by six days of cultivation under a photoperiod of 14 hours light:10 hours dark. On the ninth day, growth parameters of both peas and Chinese cabbage exposed to different concentrations of CD solutions were measured.

Peas and Chinese cabbage were treated with a 500 mg/L CD solution and deionized water. Following treatment, the plants were rinsed with deionized water, and their roots, stems and leaves were excised for sectioning. The prepared plant sections were subsequently examined under a laser confocal microscope to evaluate the uptake and translocation of CDs within the plant tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of acute toxicity of CDs to earthworms
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental procedure adhered to the Test guidelines on environmental safety assessment for chemical pesticides. Part 15: Earthworm acute toxicity test (National Public Service Platform for Standards Information, 2025). Initially, earthworms were carefully cleaned, and adult individuals exhibiting robust vitality, uniform size and a well-developed clitellum were selected for the study. Following cleaning, the earthworms were transferred to a 12 cm× 12 cm petri dish lined with filter paper. A small quantity of deionized water was added to maintain humidity, after which the dish was sealed with a transparent membrane to ensure an appropriate microenvironment. Subsequently, the petri dishes were incubated in a greenhouse maintained at 25°C under complete darkness for 24 hours to facilitate gut clearance.

CD solutions at concentrations of 100, 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, and 4000 mg/L were prepared and stored for subsequent use. Fifty-milliliter beakers were prepared by lining them with filter paper, followed by the addition of 1 mL of the respective CD solution to each beaker. The beakers were gently rotated to ensure uniform distribution of the solution across the filter paper surface. Earthworms that had undergone prior gut clearance were then individually introduced into the beakers. Each beaker was subsequently sealed with parafilm and maintained under dark conditions to prevent light exposure during the experiment. To avoid potential interference between earthworms, only one worm was placed in each beaker. Ten replicates were performed for each concentration, with deionized water serving as the control treatment. Following a 24-hour incubation period, the mortality of the earthworms was evaluated; worms were classified as deceased if they exhibited no response to gentle stimulation of their tails.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer

ce:section.ce:para: The Chinese cabbage plants treated with CD solutions were rinsed with deionized water, and their roots and leaves were separated and placed in individual petri dishes. The samples were then dried at 50°C. Following drying, the samples were pulverized and ground into a fine powder. Each treatment group was subjected to three replicates, and Chinese cabbage treated solely with deionized water served as the blank control group.

An accurately weighed sample of Chinese cabbage was placed in a 50 mL beaker, and 10 mL of mixed acid (HClO: HNO3=1:9) was added. The sample was then digested in a tightly sealed digestion apparatus within a fume hood at a temperature of 300°C. Digestion was considered complete when the resulting solution became clear and transparent, with no visible plant tissue remaining. Subsequently, the digest was transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask, diluted to volume with deionized water and sealed for storage.
The content of Fe element in Chinese cabbage was quantified using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry.

The chlorophyll meter is equipped with two LED light sources that emit red light (wavelength 650 nm) and infrared light (wavelength 940 nm). These light beams pass through the leaf tissue and are detected by a receiver. The resulting analog signal is subsequently amplified and converted into a digital signal, which is then processed to determine the chlorophyll content. Fresh plant leaves were positioned on the measuring device, and following calibration, the chlorophyll content within the leaves can be accurately quantified.

The roots and leaves of fresh Chinese cabbage were thoroughly cleaned, and 0.1 g of plant tissue was accurately weighed. Following treatment with liquid nitrogen, 0.9 mL of extraction buffer was added to the samples. The mixture was homogenized using a pre-cooled mortar on ice until a uniform suspension was obtained. Subsequently, the homogenate was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 minutes at 4°C in a low-temperature centrifuge. The resulting supernatant was collected and maintained on ice for further analysis. The activities of SOD, POD and CAT enzymes within the Chinese cabbage were assayed according to the protocols outlined in the kit instructions.

A 500 mg/L CDs solution and flonicamid solutions with concentrations ranging from 1000 to 4000 mg/L were prepared. Equal volumes of CD solutions and flonicamid solutions were mixed under continuous stirring, resulting in the formation of CDs-Flo nanocomposites through physical adsorption. The successful conjugation of CDs with flonicamid at varying concentrations was confirmed by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity determination of different formulations of flonicamid solution on pea aphids
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, CDs-Flo nano-formulations were synthesized at various mass ratios. A bioassay experiment was conducted on aphids to identify the formulation exhibiting the most effective insecticidal activity, thereby determining the optimal combination ratio of CDs and flonicamid. The CDs-Flo nano-formulations employed in subsequent experiments were prepared according to this optimized ratio.

Three flonicamid formulations, each containing the same active ingredient content, were prepared to evaluate their insecticidal effects on pea aphids using the leaf-dipping method. The formulations included CDs-Flo nano-preparations, commercially available 10 % flonicamid water-dispersible granules, and the original flonicamid technical material. Broad bean leaves of uniform size and free of pesticide residues were selected, immersed in a series of concentration gradients ranging from 1 to 20 mg/L for 30 s, and then removed and air-dried. Following hydration treatment, the petioles were placed in 12 cm × 12 cm culture dishes (two leaves per dish). Using a fine brush, pea aphids were gently transferred onto the treated broad bean leaves. Wingless adult aphids of consistent size were selected and inoculated onto the treated leaves, with 30 aphids per dish. Each concentration was replicated three times, and deionized water served as the control treatment. The aphids were maintained in a constant temperature culture room at 25°C under a 14 L:10D photoperiod. After 48 hours, aphid mortality was assessed; individuals were considered dead if they showed no response when their abdomens were gently touched with a brush.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of contact angles and adhesion work values of different formulations of flonicamid solution
ce:section.ce:para:
The contact angle is defined as the angle formed between the tangent at the gas-liquid interface and the solid-liquid interface within the liquid phase, (Huhtamäki et al., 2018) serving as a quantitative measure of wettability. Typically, a smaller contact angle indicates superior wetting performance of the sample. The mathematical representation of Young 's equation is as follows:
Cos θ = (γsv-γsl)/γlv

This equation, also known as the wetting equation, characterizes the relationship among solid-gas, solid-liquid, and liquid-gas interfacial tension γlv, γsv, γsl as well as contact angle. It applies to the equilibrium condition of a uniform surface in contact with a liquid and solid, where no specific interactions are present (Young, 1832).

Method for measuring contact angles: A CDs-Flo nano-preparation at an effective concentration of 50 mg/L, commercially available 10 % flonicamid water-dispersible granules, and the original flonicamid solution were prepared respectively. Broad bean leaves were trimmed to appropriate sizes, and their adaxial surfaces were mounted onto glass slide with transparent adhesive. The slides were then positioned on a contact angle measuring instrument. Using a microinjector, the test solutions were aspirated and carefully deposited onto the leaf surface in 1.5 μL aliquots. The instrument captured images of the droplets-leaves contact angles, which were subsequently analyzed via Young's equation fitting to determine the contact angles and work of adhesion.

Data processing and analysis were performed using Microsoft Excel 2016 and SPSS. Peak fitting was carried out with XPS peak41, while graphical visualization was conducted using GraphPad Prism 8.0.2 and Origin 2022b.

In this investigation, fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal synthesis utilizing Commelina communis as the carbon precursor ( Fig. 1A). To identify the optimal parameters for CD synthesis, a systematic investigation was performed by varying the carbon source amount, reaction duration, and reaction temperature. The fluorescence intensity of the resulting CDs was employed as an indicator to evaluate the synthesis efficiency under different conditions. Under the condition of considering energy-saving, the highest yield was achieved when 2 g of Commelina communis was mixed with 10 mL of distilled water in a 20 mL autoclave and subjected to a temperature of 240°C for 11 hours (Fig. S1). After purification and modification, a stable aqueous solution of carbon dots was successfully prepared. Subsequently, the solution was subjected to lyophilization, yielding approximately 0.05 g of carbon dot solid. Based on the initial mass of the starting materials, the production yield was calculated to be approximately 2.5 %.

Initially, the optimization of Commelina communis quantity was systematically investigated (Fig. S1A). Experimental results demonstrated a positive correlation between fluorescence intensity and biomass quantity up to 2 g under hydrothermal conditions at 200°C for 10 hours. However, further increase in biomass beyond this threshold resulted in no significant change in fluorescence intensity, accompanied by a marginal reduction at 3.5 g. This phenomenon can be attributed to incomplete carbonization processes caused by precursor overload, which subsequently diminishes reaction efficiency. Based on energy consumption analysis, 2 g was determined to be the optimal biomass dosage for carbon source implementation.

Subsequent optimization studies focused on reaction time parameters (Fig. S1B). Under standardized conditions employing 2 g biomass at 200°C, a temporal analysis revealed a direct correlation between fluorescence enhancement and reaction duration, achieving maximum emission intensity at 11 hours. Extended hydrothermal treatment beyond this optimal period induced structural degradation of CD surfaces, leading to significant fluorescence attenuation through quenching mechanisms. These findings establish 11 hours as the optimal reaction timeframe for achieving maximal fluorescence yield.

The final optimization parameter, reaction temperature, was systematically evaluated (Fig. S1C). Under standardized conditions of 2 g biomass in 10 mL aqueous medium with an 11 hours reaction period, thermal effects were investigated across a 160°C-280°C range. Spectroscopic analysis revealed a significant enhancement in fluorescence emission with increasing temperature. At temperatures of 240°C and 260°C, the fluorescence intensity plateaued close to its peak value, while further elevation to 280°C induced nanoparticle decomposition, leading to diminished fluorescence properties. These experimental results demonstrate that, from an energy-efficient standpoint, 240°C represents the optimal thermal condition for efficient CD synthesis.

Following synthesis under optimal conditions, the CDs were characterized for morphology and optical properties. Optical properties were examined through UV-Vis absorption spectrum, emission spectrum, excitation spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime measurements, while structural analysis was conducted using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD).

Under optimized synthesis conditions, a homogeneous yellow CD solution was successfully prepared (Fig. 1B). The solution exhibited intense fluorescence emission upon 365 nm ultraviolet excitation, demonstrating the excellent luminescent properties of the Commelina communis-derived CDs. Atomic force microscopy characterization revealed well-dispersed nanoparticles with uniform size distribution, showing an average diameter of 6.88 nm. Zeta potential measurements indicated a positive charge of + 7.29 mV (Fig. 1C), suggesting effective electrostatic stabilization through interparticle repulsion, which accounts for the colloidal stability and anti-aggregation properties of the solution.

The photoluminescent properties of CDs were systematically characterized through comprehensive optical analyses. The UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy revealed two distinct absorption bands at 260 nm and 315 nm (Fig. 1D). The former corresponds to the π-π* electronic transition of CC bonds in aromatic structures, characteristic of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Teng et al., 2014), while the latter originates from the n-π* transition of CO bonds (Hu et al., 2015). Photoluminescence studies demonstrated that the CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E). The excitation wavelength significantly influenced the fluorescence intensity, showing a maximum at 350 nm followed by a gradual decrease at longer wavelengths (Fig. 1F), indicating strong excitation wavelength dependence of the photoluminescent properties.

The observed optical behavior of CDs can be explained by their unique structural characteristics. Similar to many carbon-based nanomaterials, the photophysical properties of CDs are primarily governed by surface state effects and quantum confinement phenomena. The surface states, analogous to molecular states, combined with quantum size effects, create complex excited state configurations that account for the strong excitation wavelength dependence (Szapoczka et al., 2023). To further elucidate the photoluminescence mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime was employed (Becker, 2012; Huang et al., 2020). (Fig. 1G). Analysis of the decay kinetics revealed a fluorescence lifetime of 2.66 ns, reflecting intramolecular interaction within the CD structure and their sensitivity to environmental factors.

FTIR analysis was conducted to investigate the surface functionalization of CDs (Fig. 1L). The FTIR spectrum revealed characteristic vibrational modes corresponding to various functional groups: alkyl stretching at 2964.59 cm−1, -CC- stretching at 1652.99 cm−1, and -NH2 bending at 1581.63 cm−1. The presence of carboxyl groups was confirmed by the absorption peak at 1384.89 cm−1. The C-O stretching vibration at 1124.50 cm−1, along with the O-H stretching vibration near 3500 cm⁻¹ , confirms the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots. These spectroscopic sig natures demonstrate the coexistence of hydrophobic (alkyl chains, carbon-carbon double bonds) and hydrophilic (amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) moieties on the CD surface. The abundance of polar functional groups not only confers excellent aqueous solubility but also establishes a foundation for potential the applications in biochemical and agricultural domains.

XPS analysis was employed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical states of CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 1H) revealed three distinct peaks at 285.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 532.08 eV, corresponding to C1S, N1S and O1S core levels, respectively, with atomic percentages of 71.68 %, 10.98 %, and 17.14 %. High-resolution C1S spectra (Fig. 1I) demonstrated three chemical states: CO (287.83 eV), C-O (285.90 eV) and CC (284.68 eV). The N1S spectrum (Fig. 1J) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 401.73 eV (C-N-C) and 399.33 eV (N-H), while the O1S spectrum revealed contributions from C-OH (532.18 eV) and CO (531.13 eV) species. These XPS findings, consistent with FTIR analysis, confirmed the presence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the CD surface.

The crystalline structure of CDs was investigated using XRD analysis (Fig. 1M). The diffraction pattern exhibited a broad characteristic peak centered at 2θ= 28°, corresponding to the characteristic graphitic carbon diffraction peak. The diffuse nature of this peak indicates the presence of an amorphous carbon phase, suggesting a highly disordered atomic arrangement within the CDs. These structural characteristics, combined with previous analytical results, confirm the successful synthesis of carbon nanomaterials with a graphene-like crystalline configuration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiological and environmental impact of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This study employed peas and Chinese cabbage as model plants to investigate the physiological impacts of CD treatment. Comprehensive analyses were conducted on chlorophyll content, Fe content, and protective enzyme activities to elucidate CD-induced alterations in plant growth and physiological responses. The intracellular distribution of CDs within plant tissues was systematically characterized. Concurrently, earthworms were utilized as bioindicators to assess the environmental biosafety and ecotoxicological effects of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Seedlings of peas and Chinese cabbage were subjected to CD treatments at concentrations ranging from 10 to 2000 mg/L. As illustrated in Fig. 2Ⅰ, pea seedlings exhibited no significant alterations in plant height or root length, notable leaf biomass enhancement was observed. In contrast, Chinese cabbage demonstrated significant improvements in both root elongation and shoot growth, accompanied by enhanced leaf development ( Fig. 3Ⅰ). These findings indicate that CD treatment exerts species-specific growth stimulation, with a more substantial positive impact on Chinese cabbage than peas.

Quantitative growth analysis was performed to evaluate the effects of CD treatment on root length, shoot height, and biomass accumulation in both plant species. For peas, no significant differences were observed in root elongation or shoot height compared with control group across all CD concentrations (Fig. 2Ⅱ). However, a marked increase in fresh weight was detected at CD concentrations between 100 and 500 mg/L. As biomass accumulation serves as a crucial physiological indicator of plant growth, these results demonstrate that CD solutions can significantly enhance the growth within specific concentration ranges.

Fig. 3Ⅱ presents the quantitative analysis of Chinese cabbage growth parameters following CD treatment. The data demonstrate concentration-dependent responses: root elongation was significantly enhanced at 10–500 mg/L CD concentrations, while shoot height showed marked improvement at 200–500 mg/L. Biomass accumulation exhibited a substantial increase across a broader concentration range of 200–2000 mg/L. These results indicate that the CD solutions exert significant growth-promoting effects on Chinese cabbage, with optimal enhancement observed at 500 mg/L. The pronounced root development, particularly crucial for nutrient and water acquisition (Wang et al., 2018), suggests that CD-mediated root elongation may represent a fundamental mechanism underlying the observed growth stimulation in Chinese cabbage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and transport of CDs in plants
ce:section.ce:para: The uptake and translocation of CDs in plant tissues were investigated using laser scanning confocal microscopy with 405 nm excitation. Fig. 4Ⅰ presents cross-sectional images of pea roots, stems, and leaves following treatment with 500 mg/L CD solution. Comparative analysis between CD-treated samples (Fig. 4Ⅰ-A, B, C) and control groups cultured in distilled water. (Fig. 4Ⅰ-X, Y, Z) revealed distinct tissue morphology. Notably, characteristic blue fluorescence signals were detected in leaf (Fig. 4Ⅰ-F), stem (Fig. 4Ⅰ-I), and root (Fig. 4Ⅰ-L) tissues, demonstrating successful CD absorption and systemic translocation throughout the plant vascular system.

Fig. 4Ⅱ presents confocal microscopy images of Chinese cabbage tissues following 500 mg/L CD treatment, with Fig. 4Ⅱ-X, Y, Z representing the hydroponic control group. Distinct CD fluorescence signals were observed in leaf (Fig. 4Ⅱ-C), stem (Fig. 4Ⅱ-F), and root (Fig. 4Ⅱ-I) tissues, confirming CD uptake and systemic translocation. Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity revealed a concentration gradient, with the strongest signal in root tissues, followed by stems and leaves. This spatial distribution pattern demonstrates a root-to-shoot translocation pathway, suggesting that CDs are primarily absorbed through the root system and subsequently transported acropetally through the vascular system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination results of acute toxicity of CDs to earthworms
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the environmental safety of CDs, earthworms were employed as model organisms for acute toxicity evaluation across a concentration range of 100–4000 mg/L. The experimental results ( Fig. 5A), demonstrated complete survival of earthworms at all tested concentrations, establishing that the acute median lethal concentration (LC50) of CDs exceeds 4000 mg/L. Based on the chemical toxicity classification system presented in Table 1 (Kai, 2013), these findings indicate that CDs exhibit negligible acute toxicity to earthworms，suggesting their minimal environmental impact at the tested concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe element content and chlorophyll content in plants
ce:section.ce:para: Fe plays a crucial role in plant photosynthetic processes. To investigate the influence of CDs on plant photosynthesis, the content of Fe in roots, stems and leaves of Chinese cabbage was quantitatively analyzed using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Experimental data (Fig. 5B) revealed that exposure to 500 mg/L CDs solution resulted in increased Fe accumulation across all plant tissues, with the most pronounced enhancement observed in stems. This demonstrates that CDs facilitate Fe uptake in plants. The observed phenomenon may be attributed to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on the CD surface, which enhance water molecules transport into plant tissues. Since water uptake is intrinsically linked to nutrient absorption, the presence of CDs consequently improves the plant's capacity for trace element assimilation.

Leaf chlorophyll content in Chinese cabbage was quantitatively assessed using a chlorophyll meter. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, exposure to 500 mg/L CD solution resulted in a statistically significant increase in chlorophyll content relative to the control group, observed at both 5-day and 10-day intervals. These findings indicate that CDs enhance chlorophyll biosynthesis in Chinese cabbage leaves, thereby potentially improving photosynthesis efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination results of protective enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: The enzymatic responses of Chinese cabbage to CD treatment are presented in Fig. 5D-F. CD exposure significantly enhanced SOD activity in root tissues, while leaf SOD levels remained unchanged (Fig. 5D), suggesting CD-induced improvement in superoxide anion (O2-) scavenging capacity specifically in roots. This enhancement potentially mitigates abiotic stress and delays senescence in Chinese cabbage. Furthermore, CD treatment markedly increased POD activity both in roots and leaves (Fig. 5E), indicating enhanced reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging and accelerated cellular metabolism. Similarly, CAT activity showed significant upregulation in all examined tissues (Fig. 5F), demonstrating CD-mediated improvement in free radical elimination and metabolic processes, thereby enhancing the plant's overall antioxidant capacity and stress tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity assessment of different formulations of flonicamid solution on pea aphid
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibits a concentration-dependent decrease upon interaction with varying concentrations of flonicamid solution, demonstrating the occurrence of fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon, characterized by reduced fluorescence intensity and shortened luminescence lifetime, results from molecular interactions between the fluorophores and quencher molecules. The observed quenching effect provides evidence for the successful formation of CDs-flonicamid complexes.

To optimize the efficacy of CDs-Flo nanoformulations, the influence of mass ratios between CDs and flonicamid on pea aphid mortality was systematically evaluated. Preliminary experiments established the lethal concentration of flonicamid at approximately 10 mg/L. Under a fixed volume ratio of 1:1, the concentration of CDs was adjusted to examine the impact of varying mass ratios on insecticidal activity. As illustrated in Fig. S2B, pea aphid mortality exhibited a biphasic response, initially increasing and subsequently decreasing with higner CD-to-flonicamid ratios. The maximum mortality rate of 55.13 % was observed at a 1:5 mass ratio. Based on these findings, the 1:5 mass ratio was identified as the optimal formulation for CDs-Flo nanoformulations, demonstrating enhanced insecticidal performance.

The insecticidal efficacy of CDs-Flo nanocomposites was evaluated against pea aphids in comparison with commercial 10 % flonicamid water dispersible granules and pure flonicamid. Toxicity bioassays demonstrated excellent linear correlations for all formulations, with correlation coefficients of 0.983, 0.983, and 0.981, respectively ( Table 2). The CDs-Flo nanocomposite exhibited superior insecticidal activity, as evidenced by its significantly lower LC50 (8.988 mg/L) compared with water-dispersible granules (10.294 mg/L) and pure flonicamid (12.133 mg/L). This enhanced pesticidal performance can be attributed to the unique physicochemical properties of CDs, including their nanoscale dimensions, surface hydrophilic groups, and superior adhesion capabilities, Furthermore, the excellent biocompatibility and enhanced permeability of CDs facilitate improved foliar absorption and translocation of flonicamid, resulting in increased pesticide utilization efficiency and biological efficacy.

Wettability analysis of three flonicamid formulations on broad bean leaves revealed significant differences in contact angles. The CDs-Flo nanocomposite demonstrated superior wettability with a contact angle of 50.537 ± 1.680°, compared with 52.989 ± 2.933° for water dispersible granules and 57.063 ± 0.050° for pure flonicamid ( Table 3). Statistical analysis indicated a significant reduction in contact angle for the nanocomposite relative to the pure drug, with a moderate improvement over the commercial formulation. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the enhanced wettability of the CDs-Flo nanocomposite facilitates greater foliar coverage, thereby increasing the effective contact area between the pesticide and leaf surface. This improved interfacial interaction enhances the probability of aphid exposure and subsequent ingestion of the active compound.

Adhesion kinetics analysis revealed distinct performance characteristics among the three formulations (Fig. 6D). The CDs-Flo nanocomposite demonstrated significantly enhanced adhesion work compared with pure flonicamid throughout the experimental duration. While initial adhesion values were comparable to water-dispersible granules, the nanocomposite exhibited progressively superior performance over time. These results indicate that CDs-Flo formulation possesses enhanced foliar affinity and adhesion properties, which contribute to improved retention on leaf surfaces. The increased interfacial stability reduces solution runoff and minimizes pesticide loss, thereby enhancing formulation efficiency and biological efficacy.

This study developed a novel CD synthesis method utilizing Commelina communis as a sustainable carbon through a one-step hydrothermal approach. Comprehensive characterization of the synthesized CDs was conducted to elucidate their physicochemical properties, followed by systematic investigation of their phytostimulatory activity and underlying mechanisms. Furthermore, a CDs-Flo nanoformulation was engineered, and its pesticidal efficacy was comparatively evaluated against conventional flonicamid formulations. These findings provide fundamental insights into the potential agricultural applications of CDs, particularly in plant growth promotion and targeted pesticide delivery systems.

Yang Ni: Methodology, Conceptualization. Lai Jie: Methodology, Conceptualization. Tang Jiaxin: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xu Yanqiu: Validation, Software. Chen Lihan: Visualization, Investigation. Zhang Zan: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Bian Qiang: Supervision. Qian Kun: Supervision. Zhang Huanhuan: Writing – original draft, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Programme (2023YFC2604800), China Agricultural Research System (CARS-21), and Tibet Autonomous Region Key Research and Development Programme (XZ202301YZ0014N).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2025.120836.
